symptoms: Engine cranks but does not fire
No spark at spark plug
Fuel in tank but engine does not run
Recoil cord pulls but nothing happens
Engine starts then stalls immediately
causes: Fouled or worn spark plug
Stale or contaminated fuel
Blocked carburetor jets
Low engine oil triggering oil-level shutdown
Faulty ignition coil or kill switch stuck in off position
inspection_steps: Check the fuel level and verify fuel is fresh and correct grade.
Remove the spark plug and inspect for fouling, cracks, or wear; check the gap.
Check engine oil level; low oil trips the automatic shutdown on most generators.
Inspect the air filter for blockage and the carburetor bowl for dirt or water.
Verify the fuel valve is open and the choke is in the correct start position.
repair_steps: Replace a fouled or worn spark plug with the manufacturer-specified type and gap.
Drain stale fuel and refill with fresh petrol; add a fuel stabiliser for storage.
Clean the carburetor by removing the bowl and clearing jets with carb cleaner.
Top up engine oil to the correct level as shown on the dipstick.
Check and clean or replace the air filter if it is clogged.
Test the ignition coil with a spark tester; replace if no spark with a new plug.
safety: Never operate a generator indoors or in an enclosed space due to carbon monoxide risk.
Allow the engine to cool before inspecting the spark plug or fuel system.
Keep fuel away from heat, sparks, and open flames during inspection.
Disconnect the spark plug lead before working on the carburetor.
prevention: Run the generator under load for 30 minutes monthly to keep the carburetor clean.
Use fuel stabiliser in stored fuel.
Service spark plug and air filter annually.
estimated_cost: Low to moderate depending on parts

symptoms: Battery not reaching full charge during the day
Inverter shows low or zero input from panels
Charge controller shows low panel voltage
Output drops significantly compared to rated watts
System works in morning but not afternoon
causes: Partial shading from trees, buildings, or dirt on panels
Dirty or dusty panel surface reducing light absorption
Faulty or degraded bypass diode in a panel
Loose or corroded wiring connections
Panel orientation or tilt angle not optimised for season
inspection_steps: Measure open-circuit voltage across each panel with a multimeter in direct sunlight.
Inspect panel surfaces for dirt, bird droppings, cracks, or shading objects.
Check all wiring connections at the combiner box, charge controller, and panels.
Verify charge controller settings match the battery bank type and capacity.
Check for partial shading at different times of day to identify obstruction patterns.
repair_steps: Clean panels with water and a soft cloth or sponge; avoid abrasive materials.
Trim or remove shade-causing vegetation or reposition panels away from shadows.
Tighten or replace corroded MC4 connectors and check junction box seals.
Replace a panel with a failed bypass diode; check with IV-curve tracer if available.
Adjust tilt angle seasonally for maximum sun exposure at your latitude.
Reconfigure partial-shading panels with micro-inverters or power optimisers.
safety: Solar panels produce voltage in any light; treat all wiring as live.
Disconnect the battery before working on wiring to avoid arc flash.
Work on panels early morning or late afternoon when output is lower.
Do not stand on panels; use appropriate roof-access safety equipment.
prevention: Clean panels quarterly.
Trim vegetation near panels each season.
Inspect wiring connections annually.
estimated_cost: Low unless panels or diodes need replacement

symptoms: Unit runs but blows warm or room-temperature air
Compressor cycles on and off rapidly
Ice forming on indoor unit or refrigerant lines
Unusual noise from outdoor unit
High electricity bill with no cooling improvement
causes: Low refrigerant charge due to leak
Dirty air filter blocking airflow through evaporator
Dirty condenser coils preventing heat rejection outdoors
Faulty or stuck expansion valve
Compressor failure or capacitor fault
inspection_steps: Check and replace the indoor air filter if dirty or clogged.
Inspect the outdoor condenser unit for blocked coils, debris, or vegetation.
Check the thermostat settings and verify it is set to cool mode below room temperature.
Listen for the compressor clicking on; if silent with fan running, suspect capacitor.
Look for ice on the indoor unit indicating low airflow or low refrigerant.
repair_steps: Replace or clean the air filter to restore evaporator airflow.
Clean condenser coils with a coil cleaner and rinse with low-pressure water.
Remove ice by turning off cooling and running fan-only mode until ice melts.
Test and replace the start or run capacitor if the compressor does not engage.
Contact a certified HVAC technician for refrigerant recharge or compressor replacement.
safety: Disconnect power at the breaker before opening any AC panels.
Capacitors retain charge after power-off; discharge before touching.
Refrigerant handling requires EPA 608 certification; do not release to atmosphere.
Allow the outdoor unit to be clear of obstructions by at least 60 cm.
prevention: Replace air filters monthly or quarterly.
Clean condenser coils annually before summer.
Service the system with a certified technician every 1-2 years.
estimated_cost: Low for filters; high for refrigerant recharge or compressor

symptoms: Starter motor turns but engine does not fire
No power when key is turned on
Engine kicks but does not run
Engine starts briefly then dies
Clicking sound from starter circuit
causes: Dead or weak battery
Flooded engine from excessive choke use
Blocked carburetor or injector
Faulty kill switch or side-stand interlock engaged
Spark plug fouled or worn
inspection_steps: Check that the kill switch is in the RUN position and the side-stand is up.
Test battery voltage with a multimeter; should be above 12.4V.
Remove the spark plug and check for fouling, wetness, or worn electrode.
Smell the exhaust or air box for petrol indicating a flooded engine.
Check the fuel tap position and confirm there is fuel in the tank.
repair_steps: Set the kill switch to RUN, lift the side-stand, and engage the clutch if required.
Charge or replace the battery if voltage is below 12V.
Clear a flooded engine by holding the throttle wide open while cranking for 5-10 seconds.
Replace a fouled spark plug with the correct type and gap for the engine.
Clean or rebuild the carburetor if the engine runs roughly or only with choke.
safety: Keep fuel away from sparks during inspection.
Do not crank continuously for more than 10 seconds to avoid starter damage.
Ensure the motorcycle is in neutral or clutch is held before starting.
Allow the engine to reach operating temperature before hard riding.
prevention: Service the spark plug and air filter every 5,000-10,000 km.
Keep the battery on a tender during storage.
Drain the carburetor float bowl during long storage periods.
estimated_cost: Low unless battery or carburetor replacement is needed

symptoms: Fan runs loudly at high speed constantly
System throttles performance and becomes slow
Laptop shuts off suddenly under load
Bottom or keyboard area is very hot to touch
CPU temperature above 90 degrees Celsius in task manager
causes: Clogged heatsink or fan blocked by dust
Dried-out thermal paste between CPU and heatsink
Blocked ventilation slots due to soft surface use
Failing or stuck cooling fan
Background software causing excessive CPU load
inspection_steps: Install a temperature monitor and check CPU and GPU temperatures at idle and under load.
Listen for the fan; if silent under load, the fan may have failed.
Inspect ventilation slots for dust buildup using a flashlight.
Check Task Manager or Activity Monitor for processes consuming excessive CPU.
Confirm the laptop is used on a hard flat surface and not on a bed or carpet.
repair_steps: Use compressed air to blow dust out of ventilation slots and fan grilles.
Open the bottom panel, remove the fan and heatsink, and clean thoroughly.
Apply fresh high-quality thermal paste to the CPU after removing the old paste.
Replace a seized or failing fan with the manufacturer part number.
End runaway processes and disable startup programs consuming unnecessary CPU.
safety: Power off and unplug the laptop; remove the battery if removable.
Ground yourself before touching internal components to prevent static damage.
Do not apply thermal paste to surrounding components or over-tighten screws.
Allow the system to cool fully before disassembly.
prevention: Clean laptop vents with compressed air every 6 months.
Use the laptop on hard flat surfaces.
Reapply thermal paste every 2-3 years or if throttling occurs.
estimated_cost: Low unless fan replacement is required

problem: Water pump not priming or losing prime
symptoms: Pump motor runs but no water comes out
Pump delivers low flow and pressure
Pump hums loudly but stalls
Air spurting from discharge pipe intermittently
Pump works briefly then stops delivering water
causes: Air leak in suction pipe or fittings
Suction lift too high for pump design
Blocked or damaged foot valve at water source
Impeller clogged with debris or sand
Worn mechanical seal or casing cracks allowing air ingress
inspection_steps: Check all suction pipe joints and connections for air leaks by feeling for air draw or applying soapy water.
Measure the vertical distance from the water surface to the pump; most centrifugal pumps lift maximum 7-8 metres.
Remove and inspect the foot valve for debris, damage, or stuck flap.
Open the pump casing and inspect the impeller for blockage or damage.
Check the mechanical seal and casing for cracks or worn contact surfaces.
repair_steps: Seal all suction pipe joints with PTFE tape and pipe sealant; tighten all fittings.
Manually prime the pump by filling the casing and suction pipe with water before starting.
Clean or replace the foot valve if stuck, blocked, or damaged.
Remove the casing and clear impeller blockages; replace a damaged impeller.
Replace the mechanical seal if leaking air or water around the shaft.
safety: Disconnect power before opening the pump casing.
Release pressure from the discharge side before disassembly.
Wear eye protection when flushing or pressurising the system.
Ensure the pump motor is properly earthed before running.
prevention: Install a check valve on the discharge side to retain prime.
Clean the foot valve every 6 months.
Inspect suction pipe joints annually for wear.
estimated_cost: Low unless impeller or seal replacement is needed

symptoms: Battery discharges quickly after full charge
Battery voltage drops under load below 10V
White powdery sulfate deposits on plates visible through casing
Battery feels warm during charging even at low current
Charger shows full but battery fails load test
causes: Sulfation from prolonged deep discharge or storage without charge
One or more dead cells within the battery
Overcharging causing water loss and plate damage
Corroded or loose terminals increasing resistance
Age: battery has exceeded 3-5 year service life
inspection_steps: Measure open-circuit voltage after 1 hour rest; below 12.4V indicates partial discharge or degradation.
Perform a load test at half the cold-cranking amp rating; voltage should stay above 9.6V.
Inspect terminals and cables for corrosion, looseness, or heat discolouration.
Check electrolyte level in serviceable batteries; low level indicates overcharge or age.
Check specific gravity with a hydrometer if cells are accessible.
repair_steps: Clean corroded terminals with baking soda solution, wire brush, and apply terminal grease.
Attempt desulfation using a dedicated pulse charger if the battery is mildly sulfated.
Slow-charge a deeply discharged battery at 10% of its Ah rating for 10-16 hours.
Replace a battery that fails a load test, has dead cells, or is beyond 5 years old.
Check and calibrate the charging voltage to prevent overcharging; float should be 13.6-13.8V.
safety: Batteries produce hydrogen gas; avoid sparks, flames, and smoking near them.
Wear eye protection and gloves when handling electrolyte.
Dispose of old batteries at an authorised recycling facility.
Disconnect the negative terminal first and reconnect it last.
prevention: Recharge batteries before they drop below 50% state of charge.
Keep terminals clean and coated with grease.
Store batteries fully charged and on a tender during long periods of non-use.
estimated_cost: Low for cleaning; high if replacement is required

problem: Crop stunted growth and poor yield
symptoms: Plants are smaller than expected at this growth stage
Leaves are pale green or yellow
Roots are short, brown, or rotting
Fruit or grain is small and under-filled
Growth stops at a specific stage
causes: Soil nutrient deficiency especially nitrogen or phosphorus
Incorrect soil pH blocking nutrient uptake
Root damage from nematodes, grubs, or soil compaction
Inadequate moisture from drought or poor irrigation timing
Wrong variety planted for the season, climate, or soil type
inspection_steps: Dig up a plant and inspect roots for colour, length, and signs of rot or pests.
Test soil pH; most crops need 6.0-7.0; outside this range nutrients become unavailable.
Compare affected plants to healthy neighbours to identify a pattern by field location.
Review planting date, seed variety, and fertilizer records.
Count plant population per square metre to check if spacing and germination were adequate.
repair_steps: Apply a balanced fertilizer or targeted nutrient based on visible deficiency symptoms.
Correct soil pH with lime if too acid or sulphur if too alkaline according to soil test.
Irrigate consistently to field capacity without waterlogging; use mulch to retain moisture.
Remove and dispose of severely infested plants to prevent pest spread.
Consult an agricultural extension officer for seed variety recommendations for next season.
tools: soil pH meter or test kit
safety: Wear gloves and wash hands after handling fertilizers and pesticides.
Follow label rates; over-applying fertilizer can burn crops and contaminate water.
Keep children and animals away from treated fields as directed on product labels.
prevention: Rotate crops each season to break pest and disease cycles.
Test soil before each season and correct pH early.
Select certified seed varieties suited to local conditions.
estimated_cost: Variable by crop area and inputs required

problem: Cattle showing signs of illness and lethargy
symptoms: Animal is dull, separated from herd, and not eating
Temperature above 39.5 degrees Celsius
Diarrhoea or constipation
Laboured breathing or nasal discharge
Swollen joints, lameness, or reluctance to stand
causes: Bacterial infection such as pneumonia or blackleg
Tick-borne disease such as East Coast Fever or anaplasmosis
Internal parasite burden causing anaemia and weakness
Nutritional deficiency particularly in dry seasons
Plant toxicity from ingesting toxic vegetation
inspection_steps: Take rectal temperature; above 39.5 indicates infection in cattle.
Check mucous membranes for pale gums indicating anaemia from worms or tick fever.
Inspect skin and hair coat for ticks, especially in the ears and udder folds.
Check faeces for blood, mucus, or parasites.
Review vaccination and deworming history for the herd.
repair_steps: Isolate the sick animal immediately to prevent disease spread.
Provide clean water and high-quality roughage; force fluids if dehydrated.
Contact a veterinarian for diagnosis and prescription of antibiotics or antiparasitic treatment.
Apply a registered pour-on or spray tickicide if heavy tick infestation is present.
Deworm the herd using an appropriate anthelmintic if parasite burden is confirmed.
safety: Restrain the animal safely before examination to prevent injury.
Wear gloves when handling sick animals or faeces; some diseases are zoonotic.
Dispose of dead animals according to local regulations to prevent disease spread.
Do not administer prescription drugs without veterinary guidance.
prevention: Vaccinate against common local diseases annually.
Deworm on a strategic schedule based on local parasite risk.
Dip or spray cattle regularly for tick control.
estimated_time: Immediate triage; treatment over several days
estimated_cost: Variable by diagnosis and treatment required

problem: Wi-Fi router not providing internet access
symptoms: Devices connect to Wi-Fi but browser shows no internet
Router power light on but WAN or internet light is off or red
All devices affected simultaneously
Ping to 8.8.8.8 fails but ping to router gateway succeeds
DNS resolution fails but direct IP access works
causes: ISP outage or WAN cable disconnected from router
Router firmware bug causing DHCP or NAT failure
Incorrect DNS settings after configuration change
PPPoE or authentication credentials expired or wrong
Overheated router causing internal component failure
inspection_steps: Check the router's WAN port LED; if off or red, the ISP link is down.
Verify the WAN cable is securely connected from the router to the modem or ISP port.
Access the router admin page and check WAN status and IP assignment.
Try restarting the router and modem in sequence: modem first, then router.
Test from a device connected by ethernet cable to rule out Wi-Fi as the cause.
repair_steps: Power cycle the modem and router: unplug both, wait 30 seconds, restart modem first.
Check WAN cable connections at both ends and replace if damaged.
Log into router admin, navigate to WAN settings, and re-enter PPPoE credentials if required.
Reset DNS to 8.8.8.8 and 8.8.4.4 if resolution is failing after ISP line is confirmed up.
Update router firmware if a known bug is causing the issue.
Replace the router if it overheats or shows hardware failure after all software steps fail.
safety: Do not reset the router to factory defaults unless you have the ISP credentials and config noted.
Keep the router in a ventilated location away from direct heat.
Change the default admin password immediately on any new or reset router.
prevention: Keep router firmware updated.
Position router in a well-ventilated area.
Use a UPS to protect against power surge damage.
estimated_cost: Free unless hardware replacement is required

symptoms: Printer stops mid-print and shows jam error
Paper visible stuck inside the printer
Printer makes grinding or crunching noise during feed
Multiple sheets feed together causing a jam
Printer reports jam but no paper is visible
causes: Torn paper fragment left inside the printer from a previous jam
Paper loaded incorrectly or wrong paper size selected
Worn pick-up rollers failing to grip paper cleanly
Damp or curled paper sticking together during feed
Foreign object inside paper path
inspection_steps: Open all access panels and trays; look for paper fragments along the entire paper path.
Check the input tray for correct paper loading, correct guides, and paper size match.
Inspect the pick-up rollers for wear, glaze, or damage.
Check paper condition; damp or very curled paper causes multi-feed jams.
Run a printer self-test page to confirm the jam is cleared.
repair_steps: Power off the printer before clearing any jam to prevent further damage.
Pull jammed paper slowly and evenly in the direction of the paper path to avoid tearing.
Remove all torn pieces; even a small fragment will cause another jam.
Clean worn pick-up rollers with isopropyl alcohol on a lint-free cloth.
Fan and straighten paper before loading; store paper in a dry sealed package.
safety: Power off the printer before reaching inside the paper path.
Allow the fuser unit to cool for at least 5 minutes before touching; it operates at high temperature.
Do not use sharp metal tools inside the printer that could scratch drums or rollers.
prevention: Use good quality, dry paper within the printer's supported weight range.
Clean pick-up rollers every 6 months.
Never overload the paper tray beyond the marked capacity.
